content: In Romans 8:28, "all things work together for good to those who love God." What does the Greek word for "work together" mean?
explanation: Sunergeo (συνεργέω) means "to work together," all things working together for our good.

content: In Romans 12:2, "do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind." What does the Greek word for "transformed" mean?
explanation: Metamorphoo (μεταμορφόω) means "to transform from within," like a butterfly transforming from a caterpillar.

content: Romans 3:23 says who has sinned?
explanation: Romans 3:23 - For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. No exceptions.

content: According to Romans 5:8, when did God show His love for us?
explanation: Romans 5:8 - While we were still sinners, Christ died for us. Unconditional love.

content: Romans 6:23 says: "The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is ___"
explanation: Romans 6:23 - Sin leads to death, but God's free gift is eternal life through Jesus.

content: According to Romans 8:38-39, what can separate us from the love of God?
explanation: Romans 8:38-39 - Nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus.
options: Nothing - neither death, nor life, nor anything

content: Romans 10:9 names what two conditions for salvation?
explanation: Romans 10:9 - If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved.
options: Confessing Jesus as Lord with the mouth and believing He rose

content: Romans 12:2 urges us not to conform to this world but to be renewed how?
explanation: Romans 12:2 - Transformation begins with the mind. When the mind is renewed by God's Word, behavior changes.

content: Paul declared: "I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the ___ of God for the salvation of everyone who believes"
explanation: Romans 1:16 - The gospel is the power (dunamis) of God for salvation.

content: Having been justified by faith, we have ___ with God
explanation: Romans 5:1 - Justification by faith brings peace with God. Sin is wiped away.

content: Romans 5:3-4: Suffering produces endurance, endurance produces ___, and that produces hope
explanation: Hardship is the process that builds character and steadfast hope.

content: Paul asks: Should we go on sinning so that grace may abound?
explanation: Romans 6:2 - Grace is no excuse to sin. The true believer has died to sin.
options: By no means! We have died to sin

content: Romans 8:31: "If God is for us, who can be ___ us?"
explanation: If God stands with us, no one can successfully oppose us.

content: Paul exhorts believers to present their bodies as what?
explanation: Romans 12:1 - True worship is offering one's whole life to God, not just at church.
options: A living and holy sacrifice, pleasing to God (spiritual worship)

content: What does Paul say he is not ashamed of?
explanation: Romans 1:16: The gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.

content: According to Romans 3:23, what is true of all people?
explanation: Romans 3:23: All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.
options: All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God

content: By what are we justified?
explanation: Romans 5:1: Having been justified by faith, we have peace with God.

content: What are the wages of sin?
explanation: Romans 6:23: The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life.

content: What is true of those who are in Christ Jesus?
explanation: Romans 8:1: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

content: Paul calls believers to present their bodies as what?
explanation: Romans 12:1: Present your bodies as a living and holy sacrifice, pleasing to God.

content: Why is the wrath of God revealed from heaven against all ungodliness?
explanation: Romans 1:18: Because they suppress the truth by their unrighteousness.
options: They are unfaithful to their countrymen

content: How was Abraham justified according to Romans 4?
explanation: Romans 4:3-5: Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness.

content: Adam and Christ are compared to show what?
explanation: Romans 5:18-19: Through the obedience of one man (Christ), many are made righteous.
options: The Law is better than grace
Sin came through one man; righteousness through one man

content: What inner conflict does Romans 7 describe?
explanation: Romans 7:22-25: I delight in the law of God in my inner being, but I see another law in my members.
options: The flesh vs. the law of the mind

content: How does the Spirit help our weakness in prayer?
explanation: Romans 8:26: The Spirit intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words.
options: By interceding with groanings too deep for words

content: What does God's election (Jacob/Esau) emphasize?
explanation: Romans 9:11-16: Not because of works, but because of Him who calls — to display God's purpose.

content: What is the condition for salvation according to Romans 10:9-10?
explanation: Romans 10:9-10: If you confess with your mouth and believe in your heart, you will be saved.
options: Confess with the mouth, believe with the heart

content: What does the image of grafting in the olive tree teach?
explanation: Romans 11:17-24: Wild branches are grafted into the cultivated root — Gentiles share by faith.
options: The Jewish people are cut off
A new tree replaces the old

content: How does Romans 13 teach about our relationship with governing authorities?
explanation: Romans 13:1-7: Authority comes from God; pay taxes and show honor.
options: Submit, for they are God's servants

content: What does the "golden chain" of salvation (foreknew, predestined...) teach?
explanation: Romans 8:29-30: Those whom He foreknew He also predestined... called, justified, and glorified.
options: Grace is for one nation only

content: What do the "vessels of wrath" and "vessels of mercy" highlight?
explanation: Romans 9:22-24: God displays His glory upon vessels of mercy, which He prepared beforehand for glory.
options: The strength of the Jewish people

content: What does the doxology at the end of chapter 11 highlight about God?
explanation: Romans 11:33-36: From Him and through Him and to Him are all things; to Him be glory forever.
options: His infinite wisdom, judgment, and glory

content: What does Paul teach about the one weak in faith?
explanation: Romans 14:1-4: Do not pass judgment on the weak in faith; each stands or falls before the Lord.
options: Do not pass judgment; God can make him stand

content: What is Paul's missionary motto in Romans 15?
explanation: Romans 15:20-21: Not to build on another's foundation, but to go where Christ has not been named.
options: Preach the gospel where no one has preached it

content: How does Paul address the Roman believers in Romans 1:7?
explanation: Romans 1:7 — 'To all those in Rome who are loved by God and called to be saints.'
options: Loved by God and called to be saints

content: According to Romans 1:20, through what are God's invisible attributes perceived?
explanation: Romans 1:20 — 'For his invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made.'

content: What does Romans 2:1 warn about those who judge others?
explanation: Romans 2:1 — 'Therefore you have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges. For in passing judgment on another you condemn yourself, because you, the judge, practice the very same things.'
options: Condemn themselves; for they do the same things

content: What does Romans 2:11 say about God in relation to people?
explanation: Romans 2:11 — 'For God shows no partiality.'

content: Romans 3:10-12 quotes Scripture about humanity: 'None ___'
explanation: Romans 3:10-12 — 'None is righteous, no, not one... no one does good, not even one.'

content: According to Romans 4:16, by what is the promise to Abraham's offspring given?
explanation: Romans 4:16 — 'That is why it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his offspring.'

content: Through whom does Romans 5:5 say God's love is poured out into our hearts?
explanation: Romans 5:5 — 'God's love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit who has been given to us.'
options: Through the Holy Spirit who has been given

content: What does Romans 6:4 say baptism symbolizes?
explanation: Romans 6:4 — 'We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life.'
options: Buried with Christ in death and walking in newness of life

content: Romans 7:7 asks: 'Is the law sin?' — How does Paul answer?
explanation: Romans 7:7 — 'By no means! Yet if it had not been for the law, I would not have known sin. For I would not have known what it is to covet if the law had not said, You shall not covet.'
options: By no means! Through the law I came to know sin

content: According to Romans 8:14-17, those led by the Spirit are what?
explanation: Romans 8:14 — 'For all who are led by the Spirit of God are sons of God.'
options: Sons of God, fellow heirs with Christ

content: Romans 8:18 compares present sufferings with what?
explanation: Romans 8:18 — 'For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us.'
options: The glory that is to be revealed (not worth comparing)

content: What deep love for Israel does Paul express in Romans 9:3?
explanation: Romans 9:3 — 'For I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers, my kinsmen according to the flesh.'
options: Wishing he himself were accursed and cut off from Christ for their sake

content: Romans 10:17 says faith comes through what?
explanation: Romans 10:17 — 'So faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ.'
options: Through hearing — and hearing through the word of Christ

content: Romans 11:6 says if salvation is by grace, then it is no longer by what?
explanation: Romans 11:6 — 'But if it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works; otherwise grace would no longer be grace.'

content: How does Romans 12:9-10 say brotherly love should be?
explanation: Romans 12:9-10 — 'Let love be genuine. Abhor what is evil; hold fast to what is good. Love one another with brotherly affection. Outdo one another in showing honor.'
options: Genuine, abhorring evil, holding to good, with brotherly affection, outdoing in honor

content: What does Romans 12:19 teach about an enemy?
explanation: Romans 12:19 — 'Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.'
options: Never avenge yourselves; leave it to the wrath of God

content: What does Romans 12:21 teach must overcome evil?
explanation: Romans 12:21 — 'Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.'

content: What is the only debt Romans 13:8 says remains?
explanation: Romans 13:8 — 'Owe no one anything, except to love each other.'
options: The debt to love one another

content: What does Romans 14:13 urge believers not to do regarding food matters?
explanation: Romans 14:13 — 'Therefore let us not pass judgment on one another any longer, but rather decide never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in the way of a brother.'
options: Not pass judgment on each other or place a stumbling block

content: Romans 15:1-2 — what should the strong do for the weak?
explanation: Romans 15:1 — 'We who are strong have an obligation to bear with the failings of the weak, and not to please ourselves.'
options: Bear with their failings and please them for their building up

content: Romans 15:13 prays God of hope to fill believers with what 'abounding by the power of the Holy Spirit'?
explanation: Romans 15:13 — 'May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, so that by the power of the Holy Spirit you may abound in hope.'

content: Whom does Paul commend in Romans 16:1-2 as 'a servant of the church at Cenchreae'?
explanation: Romans 16:1 — 'I commend to you our sister Phoebe, a servant of the church at Cenchreae.'

content: Whom does Romans 16:17 warn about in the church?
explanation: Romans 16:17 — 'I appeal to you, brothers, to watch out for those who cause divisions and create obstacles contrary to the doctrine that you have been taught; avoid them.'
options: Those who cause divisions and teach contrary to apostolic doctrine

content: What does Romans 8:9 say about anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ?
explanation: Romans 8:9 — 'Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.'

content: What does Romans 4:25 say Jesus was delivered up for, and raised for?
explanation: Romans 4:25 — 'Who was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification.'
options: For the law; for the patriarchs
For our trespasses; for our justification

content: Whom does Romans 13:14 urge believers to put on, and what to make no provision for?
explanation: Romans 13:14 — 'Put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires.'
options: Put on the law; not on possessions
Put on the priesthood; not many words
Put on the Lord Jesus Christ; make no provision for the flesh

content: In Romans 1:17 Paul writes 'the righteousness of God is revealed' in the Gospel. Which corresponding phrase echoes this at 3:21 after he has proven all are sinners?
explanation: Rom 1:17 and 3:21-22 both use 'the righteousness of God revealed/manifested'. This is the main argumentative arch of Rom 1-3: indictment (1:18-3:20) → solution announced (3:21 'But now').
options: 'But now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law, although the Law and the Prophets bear witness to it'
'Whoever boasts must boast in Christ Jesus our Lord and in nothing else'
'I am not ashamed of the Gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation'
'For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God forever'

content: According to Romans 5:18-19, Paul sets Adam in counterpart to Christ in the 'one man... many' structure. What is the core counterpart?
explanation: Rom 5:18-19 — 'as ONE trespass led to condemnation for all men, so ONE act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men'. The one-to-many parallel grounds the doctrine of representational headship.
options: Adam brought physical suffering; Christ brings physical health and wealth to his people
One trespass of Adam led to condemnation for all; one act of righteousness of Christ leads to justification
Adam was ancestor of Israel; Christ is ancestor of the Gentile peoples specifically
Adam taught the law; Christ taught the gospel and abolished the law completely

content: Two famous verses in Romans — 3:23 and 6:23 — pair to form the heart of the Gospel appeal. Which two truths do they connect, in order?
explanation: Rom 3:23 — 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (diagnosis). Rom 6:23 — 'the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord' (remedy). The two link sin-judgment to gospel-salvation.
options: Anyone can believe in God on their own / Every believer will receive eternal life
Jews have a religious advantage by birth / Gentiles have a moral advantage by nature
All have sinned and fall short of God's glory / Wages of sin is death, but free gift is eternal life in Christ
The Law of Moses has fully expired / The Gospel has replaced it for all people

content: In Romans 4:9-12, Paul argues from the timing of Abraham being counted righteous (Gen 15:6) relative to circumcision (Gen 17). What is Paul's conclusion?
explanation: Rom 4:10-11 — 'How then was it counted to him? Was it before or after he had been circumcised? It was not after, but before... so that he might be the father of all who believe'. Conclusion: Gentile believers need not be circumcised.
options: Abraham was justified at the moment of circumcision, since circumcision is the covenant sign
Abraham was justified some years after circumcision, when his faith had matured
Justification and circumcision happened simultaneously, with no order between them
Abraham was justified BEFORE circumcision, so he is father of uncircumcised believers too

content: Paul uses 'But now' / 'Now' at key argumentative pivots in Romans 1-8. Which passages contain such a major pivot? (select all correct)
explanation: Rom 3:21, 5:11, 8:1 mark three argumentative pivots: from sin to solution, from justification to reconciliation, from gift to assurance. 16:25 is the closing doxology, not an argumentative pivot.
options: 3:21 — 'But now the righteousness of God has been manifested' (after the indictment)
5:11 — 'Now we have received reconciliation through him' (after justification)
8:1 — 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus'
16:25 — 'Now to him who is able to strengthen you' (closing doxology)

content: Romans 12:1 opens the practical section (12-15) with 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable'. Paul echoes the 'offering' imagery at 15:16 with what intent?
explanation: Rom 15:16 — 'Because of the grace given me by God to be a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles in the priestly service of the gospel of God, so that the offering of the Gentiles may be acceptable, sanctified by the Holy Spirit'. The worship-priestly imagery brackets the practical section (12:1 → 15:16).
options: Paul claims to be the supreme high priest of the new covenant
Paul's ministry is a 'priest of the Gospel' so that the Gentiles become an 'offering' acceptable to God
The church must offer animal sacrifices on a physical altar at the temple in Jerusalem
Every believer must give money as an 'offering' to the church in Rome above all

content: According to Romans 5:1-5, immediately upon being justified by faith, the believer receives THREE specific benefits. What are they?
explanation: Rom 5:1-2 — 'Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. Through him we have also obtained access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and we rejoice in hope of the glory of God'.
options: Material wealth / physical health / high social status in society
Abolition of Mosaic law / transfer to Gentile community / a new temple
Peace with God / access into this grace / rejoicing in the hope of glory
Avoiding bodily death / never being sick / never being tempted at all

content: In Romans 8:5-9, Paul distinguishes 'those who live according to the flesh' from 'those who live according to the Spirit'. According to the text itself, what practical mark distinguishes them?
explanation: Rom 8:5-6 — 'Those who live according to the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh, but those who live according to the Spirit set their minds on the things of the Spirit'. The mindset is the practical mark of distinction.
options: The flesh-walker SETS THEIR MIND on the flesh; the Spirit-walker SETS THEIR MIND on the Spirit — a different mindset orientation
The flesh-walker wears white robes; the Spirit-walker wears red robes in the church gathering
The flesh-walker is a Pharisee; the Spirit-walker is a Sadducee in the old society
The flesh-walker speaks Hebrew; the Spirit-walker speaks Greek in worship services

content: In Romans 15:23-29, Paul outlines his missionary travel plan. Which items ARE part of that plan? (select all correct)
explanation: Rom 15:23-29 — Paul's plan: (1) Jerusalem first with the Macedonia/Achaia collection (15:25-27), (2) then Rome (15:28-29), (3) then on to Spain (15:24, 28). Paul had no wife or children (1 Cor 7:7-8).
options: Going to Spain (Hispania) after passing through Rome on the way
Going to Jerusalem first to deliver a collection for poor saints
Visiting the Roman church on his way to Spain
Going to Israel to visit his wife and children before reaching Spain

content: Paul uses 'law' (nomos) in Romans with several distinct senses. Which senses are explicitly present in the book? (select all correct)
explanation: Paul uses nomos in multiple senses: (1) Mosaic law 7:7-12, (2) 'law of sin and death' 7:23-25, 8:2b, (3) 'law of the Spirit of life' 8:2a, sometimes 'law' = operating principle. 'Law of Babylon' is not in Romans.
options: The Law of Moses / law given at Sinai (Romans 7:7, 7:12)
The law of sin and death (Romans 7:23, 8:2)
The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:2)
The law of the kingdom of Babylon (not present in Romans)

content: According to Romans 9:6-8, Paul distinguishes two senses of 'Israel'. What is the distinction?
explanation: Rom 9:6-8 — 'For not all who are descended from Israel belong to Israel... it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as offspring'.
options: Ancient Israel and modern Israel after the post-exile restoration of statehood
'Israel according to the flesh' (Abraham's biological descendants) ≠ 'Israel of promise' (children of promise, heirs by faith)
Israel as the ten northern tribes versus Judah as the two southern tribes during the kings
Old Testament Israel and the New Testament Church are two unrelated concepts entirely

content: In Romans 11:17-24, Paul uses the 'olive tree' metaphor to warn the Gentiles. What is the specific warning?
explanation: Rom 11:18-21 — 'Do not be arrogant toward the branches... it is not you who support the root, but the root that supports you... Branches were broken off so that I might be grafted in. That is true. They were broken off because of their unbelief, but you stand fast through faith. Do not be arrogant, but fear'.
options: Eat plenty of olive fruit so that your faith may grow strong and bear much fruit
Marry only within the Jewish people to keep Abraham's line pure and undefiled
Begin keeping the Passover feast as the Jews do, although you are not required
Do not boast over the broken branches (Israel) — God can prune you off as well

content: According to Romans 1:18-20, why does Paul affirm that humanity is 'without excuse' before God's wrath?
explanation: Rom 1:19-20 — 'For what can be known about God is plain to them, because God has shown it to them. For his invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made. So they are without excuse'.
options: Because God's invisible attributes (eternal power and divine nature) HAVE BEEN CLEARLY SEEN through creation, perceivable by all
Because all have read the Law of Moses in the synagogues over many past centuries
Because Christ has come into the world, and everyone has had opportunity to meet him in person
Because God has sent prophets to every nation on earth to preach a full message

content: In Romans 3:25-26, God 'put forward' Christ Jesus as a 'propitiation' (hilastērion). What is the dual purpose of this act?
explanation: Rom 3:25-26 — '...whom God put forward as a propitiation by his blood, to be received by faith. This was to show God's righteousness... so that he might be JUST and the JUSTIFIER of the one who has faith in Jesus'. Dual purpose: God's justice upheld + sinner justified.
options: So God might gain more glory in the eyes of angels and so men might admire him more
To DEMONSTRATE that God is JUST (rightly punishes sin) AND that he JUSTIFIES the one who has faith in Jesus
To end the Old Covenant era and begin a new temple worship system entirely
So Christ might experience death as a man and understand human existence better

content: Romans 8:1 — 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus'. According to 8:2-4, what is the BASIS for this freedom from condemnation?
explanation: Rom 8:3-4 — 'For God has done what the law, weakened by the flesh, could not do. By sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and for sin, he CONDEMNED SIN in the flesh, in order that the righteous requirement of the law might be FULFILLED in us'.
options: Because the believer has personally kept the law fully to the end of life
Because the law has been entirely abolished by God and is no longer in force
Because God CONDEMNED SIN in the flesh of Christ — the law's righteous requirement is fulfilled IN him, given TO us
Because the angels protect the believer from any punishment that God might bring

content: In Romans 8:18-25, Paul writes that 'the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now'. Specifically, what is creation's groaning anticipating?
explanation: Rom 8:19-21 — 'For the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the sons of God... the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God'.
options: The final cosmic collapse into total fiery destruction with nothing left
The spread of the Gospel to all plants and animals on earth today
The return of Jewish believers to the promised land of Canaan as prophesied
The revealing of God's children (the resurrection in glory) — creation will be freed from corruption together with them

content: In Romans 12:9-21, Paul lists marks of genuine love. Which marks ARE in his list? (select all correct)
explanation: Rom 12:9-21 contains a long list of marks of genuine love: 12:9 abhor evil, 12:10 brotherly affection, 12:13 contribute to saints' needs, 12:15 weep/rejoice together, 12:17 do not repay evil, 12:20 feed the enemy... 'Only your own party' is NOT there and contradicts 12:13-14.
options: Abhor what is evil; hold fast to what is good (12:9)
Love one another with brotherly affection (12:10)
Rejoice with those who rejoice, weep with those who weep (12:15)
Only show hospitality to fellow members of your own party (not present)

content: According to Romans 13:1-5, Paul teaches the believer must submit to governing authorities for what core theological reason?
explanation: Rom 13:1-2 — 'Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed'.
options: Because 'there is no authority except from God' — governing authority is established by God
Because the Romans had the right to punish severely those who rebelled
Because the Roman officials were all believers who loved the church in Rome at that time
Because the Mosaic Law requires submission to every official on earth

content: According to Romans 13:8-10, Paul summarizes 'the law' (commandments about human relationships) by what core principle?
explanation: Rom 13:8-10 — 'For the commandments, You shall not commit adultery, You shall not murder, You shall not steal, You shall not covet... are summed up in this word: You shall love your neighbor as yourself... Therefore love is the fulfilling of the law'.
options: The Ten Commandments of Moses must be kept word-for-word with no omission allowed
One must give a tithe to God and another tithe to the church to receive blessing
'Love your neighbor as yourself' — for love is the fulfillment of the law
One must fast at least three days every week to keep the body pure from sin

content: In Romans 1:16 (ESV), Paul declares: 'I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes'. What is the order-of-priority phrase that follows?
explanation: Rom 1:16 — 'For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, TO THE JEW FIRST AND ALSO TO THE GREEK'. This reflects salvation history: the gospel came to Israel first, then opened to the Gentiles.
options: 'To the Greek first and also to the Jew' (reversing the older order)
'To the Jew first and also to the Greek'
'To the free first, and then to the slave' in ancient society
'To the rich first, and then to the poor' in the Roman community

content: According to Romans 3:23 (ESV), what is the precise wording?
explanation: Rom 3:23 (ESV) — 'For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God'. This (with 3:10 'None is righteous, no, not one') concludes the argument of 1:18-3:20.
options: 'All Israelites have sinned and forfeited their place as the chosen people of God'
'All of us are utterly lost; none of us can ever be redeemed any longer'
'Everyone has sinned at least once at some point during their life on earth'
'For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God'

content: According to Romans 6:23 (ESV), what is the complete precise wording?
explanation: Rom 6:23 (ESV) — 'For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord'. The contrast structure: 'wages' (deserved) vs. 'free gift' (grace).
options: 'For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord'
'For the Law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ'
'For everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved from the wrath to come'
'For God so loved us that he sent his Son to die for us'

content: According to Romans 8:31-32 (ESV), what rhetorical question and a-fortiori argument does Paul make to assure believers?
explanation: Rom 8:31-32 — 'What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' An a-fortiori argument (greater to lesser).
options: 'If God stands on our side then the angels will protect us from every danger on earth'
'Since God already gave us the Law of Moses, why do we still fear anything at all?'
'If God is for us, who can be against us? He did not spare his own Son but gave him for us — will he not also graciously give us all things?'
'If God truly loves us, he will surely make us materially wealthy in this present life'

content: According to Romans 10:9-10 (ESV), Paul gives a brief two-part formula for salvation. What are the two parts?
explanation: Rom 10:9-10 — 'If you CONFESS with your MOUTH that Jesus is Lord and BELIEVE in your HEART that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved. For with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved'.
options: Be baptized in water first, then offer money so that you may be saved forever
CONFESS with your MOUTH that Jesus is Lord, and BELIEVE in your HEART that God raised him from the dead
Memorize the Law of Moses and keep the Sabbath day all your life perfectly
Travel to the Jerusalem temple and offer a sacrifice at least once in your lifetime

content: According to Romans 12:2 (ESV), Paul gives two paired commands (negative + positive) for the Christian life. What are they?
explanation: Rom 12:2 — 'Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind, that by testing you may discern what is the will of God, what is good and acceptable and perfect'. Structure: negative (don't conform) + positive (be transformed) + purpose (test God's will).
options: Do not go to Israel's temple / Do go to the Roman temple of Caesar
Do not keep the Law of Moses / Do follow the local customs of the Gentiles
Do not befriend sinners / Do befriend only the priests of the temple
Do not be conformed to this world / Be transformed by the RENEWAL of your MIND
